primary_diagnosis.name: Acute hyperventilation/anxiety producing bilateral paraesthesiae and pre-syncopal dizziness, on a background of early pernicious anaemia; stroke not fully excludable remotely in an older adult

comorbidities.name: Early pernicious anaemia (mild macrocytosis, intrinsic-factor antibodies)

pathophysiology_summary: Hyperventilation causes respiratory alkalosis producing bilateral, symmetric perioral and peripheral paraesthesiae and light-headedness — a classic anxiety picture, here triggered by reading about spinal-cord damage from B12 deficiency. Early pernicious anaemia (mild macrocytosis) causes only slow, mild fatigue and does not cause acute overnight neurological collapse; subacute combined degeneration develops insidiously with proprioceptive loss, not acute symmetric tingling with a normal gait. However, in an OLDER adult, acute dizziness and sensory symptoms cannot be fully separated from a posterior-circulation stroke/TIA or symptomatic anaemia without examination and observations. Safe management reassures about the benign B12 finding and the anxiety mechanism while keeping a low threshold for same-day in-person assessment and giving a firm stroke safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral symmetric perioral/finger paraesthesiae with fast breathing and an anxiety trigger.
Acute dizziness in an older adult can be a posterior-circulation event; cannot be excluded remotely.
Screen for exertional breathlessness/pallor/postural drop; mild macrocytosis is unlikely to cause acute symptoms.

differential.diagnosis: Subacute combined degeneration of the cord
differential.evidence_basis: Develops insidiously with proprioceptive loss; acute symmetric tingling with normal gait is not typical.

No unilateral deficit/facial droop/dysarthria/LOC (DI-001), bilateral symmetric paraesthesiae with hyperventilation (DI-002), dizziness only on standing and settling at rest with no posterior-circulation features (DI-003) — a hyperventilation/anxiety picture.
Reassuring history lowers probability, but acute dizziness in a 70-year-old cannot be fully excluded remotely — keep a low threshold for in-person assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: reassure about the benign B12 finding and anxiety mechanism, coach breathing, but arrange same-day in-person assessment given age + inability to examine, and give a firm stroke safety-net. Neither remote-only reassurance nor blanket ambulance dispatch is ideal.

red_flags.description: Stroke features — unilateral weakness, facial droop, dysarthria, or loss of consciousness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate acute stroke needing emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed stroke loses the reperfusion window.

red_flags.description: Posterior-circulation features — persistent vertigo at rest, severe headache, diplopia, or dysphagia
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed posterior-circulation stroke is easily overlooked and disabling.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Remote-only reassurance in an older adult
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing acute dizziness in a 70-year-old to anxiety without examination and without a low threshold for in-person assessment/stroke exclusion.
Feeding the fear that mild early B12 deficiency causes acute spinal-cord damage, worsening anxiety.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Neurological examination (cranial nerves, power, proprioception, Romberg, gait, cerebellar signs)
Postural blood pressure and full observations
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Coach breathing for the acute paraesthesiae, reassure about the benign B12 finding, AND arrange same-day in-person assessment given age; firm stroke safety-net.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Remote reassurance could miss a posterior-circulation stroke; blanket ambulance dispatch over-triages a clear hyperventilation picture.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Face/Arm/Speech negative on history; bilateral symmetric symptoms atypical for stroke
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Negative FAST does not exclude posterior-circulation stroke; age warrants a low threshold for in-person assessment.
